evidence: The docstring defines u n = p_n/n where p_n is the nth prime and asks about the density of n with u_n < u_{n+1}.
The statement asks whether the set {n | u n < u (n+1)} has positive natural density (Set.HasPosDensity).
The docstring asks whether every convex polygon has a vertex with no other 4 vertices equidistant from it (ConvexIndep vertex sets).
The core predicate HasNEquidistantProperty is about n vertices equidistant from a given vertex of the polygon.
The statement is about p(a,d), the least prime congruent to a mod d, exceeding (1+c)*phi(d)*log d for many residues a.
The bound is measured against phi(d)*log d and the count of admissible residues is compared to phi(d) (Nat.totient in the Lean statement).
The quantity leastCongruentPrime a d (least prime in the arithmetic progression a mod d) is exactly the subject of Linnik's theorem.
The docstring asks whether there are infinitely many primes p such that floor(p*alpha) is also prime.
The hypothesis requires alpha > 1 to be irrational (Irrational α in the Lean statement).
The module header cites Turán's power-sum problems ([Tu84b] 'On a new method of analysis') and the statement bounds max_k |sum z_i^k|, the central quantity of Turán's power sum method.
The statement quantifies over sequences z_i in the complex numbers with |z_i| >= 1 and z_1 = 1.
The docstring sums tau(f(n)), 'the divisor counting function, which is σ 0 in mathlib', and asks for the asymptotic c*x*log x.
The statement is restricted to irreducible polynomials f in Z[X] (Irreducible f) that are eventually positive.
The open question concerns (k-2)-power-free values of an irreducible polynomial f of degree k > 3 with positive leading coefficient.
Part (iii) asks whether n^4 + 2 represents infinitely many squarefree numbers (Squarefree (n^4+2) in the Lean statement).
f_k(n) counts representations n = p_1^k + ... + p_k^k where the p_i are prime numbers.
Representing n as a sum of k-th powers of primes is the Waring–Goldbach setting; the problem asks if the representation count has limsup infinity.
h(n) is the minimum number of distinct distances determined by n points in general position — a variant of the Erdős distinct distances problem (not the original problem itself).
The point sets are required to be in general position: no three collinear and no four cocyclic (InGeneralPosition in the Lean statement).
